---
title: "Assignment 2"
author: "Hussain Abdul Kadhir"
date: last-modified
format:
  pdf:
    mainfont: "Papyrus"
    headings-font: "Papyrus"
---

## Data Description:
The purity of oxygen produced by a fractional distillation process is thought to be
related to the percentage of hydrocarbons in the main condensor of the processing unit.


## a. Enter the data into R and make a dataframe

```{r data}
purity <- c(86.91, 89.85, 90.28, 86.34, 92.58, 87.33, 86.29, 91.86, 95.61, 
89.86, 96.73, 99.42, 98.66, 96.07, 93.65, 87.31, 95.00, 96.85, 85.20, 90.56)

hydrocarbon <- c(1.02, 1.11, 1.43, 1.11, 1.01, 0.95, 1.11, 0.87, 1.43, 1.02, 
1.46, 1.55, 1.55, 1.55, 1.40, 1.15, 1.01, 0.99, 0.95, 0.98)

data <- data.frame(purity, hydrocarbon)

data
```

## b. Fit a simple linear regression model to the data

```{r model}


# using in built in function
model <- lm(data$purity ~ data$hydrocarbon)

# computation
n <- length(data$purity)
x <- data$hydrocarbon
y <- data$purity
syy <- sum(y**2) - ((sum(y)**2)/n)
sxx <- sum(x**2) - ((sum(x)**2)/n)
sxy <- sum(x*y) - ((sum(x)*sum(y))/n)

(beta1_hat <- sxy/sxx)
(beta0_hat <- mean(y) - beta1_hat*mean(x))

# to check the coefficients from the model
(model$coefficients)


plot(x, y)
abline(beta0_hat, beta1_hat, col = "red", lty = 2)
```
## c. Calculate $R^2$ and interpret its value.
```{r r-squared}
# sum square error
(sse <- syy - ((sxy)**2/sxx))
# sum square total
(sst <- syy)
# sum square regression
(ssr <- sst - sse)

# rsq (MSE)
(rsq <- ssr/sst)
```
:::::: {.callout-note}
R-squared value of 0.39 means the model can explain 39% of the variance. In other words, the variable hydrocarbon can explain 39% of the variability in purity. The remaining 61% of the variance is due to other factors not included in the model or random error.
:::


## d. Test the null hypothesis $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$ versus $H_1: \beta_1 \neq 0$ at $\alpha = 0.01.$

```{r testing beta_1}
(mse <- sse/(n-2))
(t_0 <- (beta1_hat/sqrt(mse/sxx)))
(p_value <- 2 * (1 - pt(abs(t_0), n-2)))
cat(p_value>0.01)
```
::: {.callout-note}
-   The p-value is less than the alpha = 0.01, hence we reject the null hypothesis. 
-   This suggests that there is a statistically Significant relationship between hydrocarbon  and purity.
-   beta1 is not equal to zero.
:::

## e. Find 95% CI on the slope 

```{r ci}
upper <- beta1_hat + sqrt(mse/sxx) * qt(0.975, n-2)
lower <- beta1_hat - sqrt(mse/sxx) * qt(0.975, n-2)
cat("95% CI for beta1: [", lower, ", ", upper, "]")
```


## f. Find s 95% CI on the mean purity when the percentage of hydrocarbons is 1.00.

![formula: 2.43](mean_ci_formula.png){width="50%"}

```{r ci_mean}
# using the formula 2.43 from the notes
mu_hat <- beta0_hat + beta1_hat * 1.00
se_mu <- sqrt(mse * (1/n + (1.00 - mean(x))**2/sxx))
upper_mu <- mu_hat + se_mu * qt(0.975, n-2)
lower_mu <- mu_hat - se_mu * qt(0.975, n-2)
cat("95% CI for mean purity when x_0 is 1.00: [", lower_mu, ", ", upper_mu, "]")
```


## g.construct the analysis of variance table


```{r anova}
# using in built function
summary(aov(model))

# we know ssr, sse, and sst

# degrees of freedom are defined as follows:
df_r <- 1
df_t <- n - 1
df_e <- df_t - df_r

# mean squares
msr <- ssr/df_r
mse <- sse/df_e

# F statistic
(F_stat <- msr/mse)

# p-value for F statistic
(p_value_f <- 1 - pf(F_stat, df_r, df_e))


# checking hypothesis that at least one predictor is significant
cat(p_value_f < 0.01)

```

::: {.callout-note}
-   Since the p-value for the F statistic is less than 0.01, we reject the null hypothesis that all regression coefficients are equal to zero. 
-   This suggests that hydrocarbon is significantly related to the response variable (purity).
:::
